scenario: Within Aurora Bank's loan onboarding flow, developers embed a Camunda 8.8 form that contains a custom submit button into a process averaging 1500 user tasks per day. A new developer asks the team lead what happens to that submit button at runtime when the linked form is rendered for a user task in Tasklist.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 runtime do with a submit button declared inside a Camunda Forms schema linked to a user task?

options.text: It hides the submit button at render time so the engine can control the moment the user task is allowed to complete from the Tasklist UI
It renders the submit button as defined but ignores its click event, leaving task completion to the standard Tasklist complete control
It rejects deployment of the linked form because user-task forms must not declare a submit button in their schema before deployment
It promotes the custom submit button to the Tasklist toolbar so the user can trigger completion from either the form or the toolbar

a.text: Correct. The Quickstart note states that any submit button present in the form schema is hidden when using Camunda Forms, so the platform can control exactly when a user is allowed to complete the task.

b.text: Incorrect. The submit button is not merely silenced behind the scenes; the Quickstart says it is hidden from the rendered form so users only see the Tasklist control for completing the task.

c.text: Incorrect. Forms with a submit button still deploy and run; the documented behaviour is hiding the button at render time, not rejecting the form during deployment.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda does not surface a per-form submit button in the Tasklist toolbar; task completion is driven by the Tasklist control after the engine signals readiness.

explanation: The Camunda Forms runtime hides any submit button declared in the form schema when the form is linked to a user task. Completion is gated by the engine through the Tasklist UI rather than by a custom button placed inside the form itself.
